questions.q: Why do closures passed to `thread::spawn` usually need the `move` keyword?
questions.a: The spawned thread may outlive the scope where the closure was created. Without `move`, the closure could hold references to local variables that get dropped — a dangling reference. `move` transfers ownership of captured values into the thread, ensuring they live as long as the thread does.

questions.q: What does `Arc<Mutex<T>>` provide that a plain `Mutex<T>` cannot?
questions.a: `Mutex<T>` alone cannot be shared across threads because it doesn't implement `Clone`. `Arc<T>` provides reference-counted, thread-safe shared ownership via atomic operations (`Arc` implements `Send` and `Sync`). Together, `Arc<Mutex<T>>` allows multiple threads to safely share and mutate the same value.

questions.q: When would you prefer channels (`mpsc`) over shared state (`Arc<Mutex<T>>`) for inter-thread communication?
questions.a: Prefer channels when threads have a clear producer/consumer relationship and data flows one-way — e.g., worker threads producing results for a single aggregator. Prefer `Arc<Mutex<T>>` when multiple threads need concurrent read/write access to the same data structure, such as a shared cache or counter.

questions.q: What prevents data races at compile time in Rust? Name the two marker traits and what each guarantees.
questions.a: `Send`: the type can be safely transferred (moved) to another thread. `Sync`: the type can be safely shared by reference (`&T`) across threads. The compiler checks these at `thread::spawn` call sites. Combined with ownership (no aliased mutable access), these make data races a compile-time error.
